glossary.definition: A layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.
glossary.sentence: It has ( Negnevitsky , 2011): ... at least one hidden layer that consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: Propagation of inputs forward in the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: The process of propagating error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer.
glossary.sentence: performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer;

glossary.definition: An algorithm that allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer to reduce the error at the output.
glossary.sentence: The backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer (Fig.

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A component of a knowledge-based intelligent system that maps objects and relations that exist in the real world to computational objects and relations, storing a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base maps objects and relations that exist in the real world to computational objects and relations.

glossary.definition: A component whose task is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a task of the inference engine is to relate production rules to find a logical reasoning chain consisting of known facts and conclusions that they support (Mallach, 1994).

glossary.definition: A storage system that stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.
glossary.sentence: The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A person who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts, emulating the human expert’s methodology and performance.
glossary.sentence: An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.
glossary.sentence: Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour.
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: Schemas that use a graph to represent knowledge where each node represents a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.
glossary.sentence: However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts.

glossary.term: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them.
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties.

glossary.definition: a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values.

glossary.definition: allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.
glossary.sentence: They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network, that is, a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network, that is, a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node.

glossary.definition: A data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation, representing typical knowledge about an object/concept or information relevant to stereotyped entities.
glossary.sentence: A frame is "a data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation" (Minsky, 1975).

glossary.definition: Properties of objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Slots are properties of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames that represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Collections of related frames linked together.
glossary.sentence: According to Minsky (1975), "collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems" .

glossary.definition: A frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: Classes derived from other classes.
glossary.sentence: Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes (Figure 5).

glossary.definition: A frame that represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain.
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: A principle used to relate two frames where a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame.
glossary.sentence: This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A relation that relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame.
glossary.sentence: Generalization relation . It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame.

glossary.definition: A relation that allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation . It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9).

glossary.definition: A relation that allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A feature allowed when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously, provided that slot titles of parent frames are unique.
glossary.sentence: Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: A demon is a procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Frames that are acquired when demons are assigned to them.
glossary.sentence: Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: A demon triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it.
glossary.sentence: IF-NEEDED - it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: A demon triggered when a slot value changes.
glossary.sentence: IF-CHANGED - it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: A demon triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: IF-ADDED - it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: A demon triggered when a slot value is deleted.
glossary.sentence: IF-REMOVED - it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: A knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules.
glossary.sentence: A rule-based expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system in which domain knowledge is represented in the form of a set of rules (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: Schemas that represent a set of problem-solving instructions not a knowledge about objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: Rule-based or procedural knowledge representation schemas are used to develop such systems. They represent a set of problem-solving instructions not a knowledge about objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: A chunk of knowledge about solving a problem.
glossary.sentence: Each production rule is a chunk of knowledge about solving a problem (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: An intelligent system acquired if statements are added to the THEN part of rules.
glossary.sentence: According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired; however, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: An intelligent system acquired if the THEN part of rules contains actions.
glossary.sentence: According to (Winston, 1992), if statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired; however, if the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: Rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts: a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.
glossary.sentence: Production systems are rule-based expert systems that consist of three parts (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2005; Negnevitsky , 2011): a knowledge base that contains a set of production rules; a working memory; an inference engine.

glossary.definition: A component that ensures the rule-based expert system keeps track of the current state of the problem-solving process by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration.
glossary.sentence: The rule-based expert system must keep track of the current state of the problem-solving process (case-specific data) and the working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A description of the current state of the problem-solving process contained in the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: An interface intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules.
glossary.sentence: Additional components, such as external databases and programs , may allow an expert system to work with external data files and program results while the developer interface is intended to edit rules in the knowledge base (add new rules as well as modify and delete existing rules) and to check the performance of the expert system after updating the rules ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Production rules in the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Production rules in the conflict set are called to be enabled.

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, which are the set of enabled rules.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set (Hopgood, 2001; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A method to fire more specific rules, based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one.
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
